expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of the structural and functional organization of the human brain—the most complex organ in the body—detailing the four major divisions: cerebrum (cerebral hemispheres, cortex, basal ganglia, limbic structures), cerebellum (motor coordination and learning), brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla oblongata—vital functions and cranial nerve nuclei), and limbic system (emotion, memory, motivation). Emphasis is placed on regional specialization (cortical lobes, functional areas), hierarchical organization (brainstem → diencephalon → cortex), protective structures (meninges, ventricles, cerebrospinal fluid), vascular supply (circle of Willis, arterial territories), and clinical correlations (stroke syndromes, movement disorders, memory disorders, brain death).

core_concept.principle: The human brain is organized hierarchically: the brainstem regulates vital autonomic functions (respiration, cardiovascular, consciousness) and serves as a conduit for ascending/descending pathways; the diencephalon (thalamus, hypothalamus) relays sensory information and maintains homeostasis; the cerebrum (cerebral cortex, basal ganglia, limbic system) enables higher cognitive functions, voluntary movement, emotion, and memory; the cerebellum coordinates movement, balance, and motor learning—with all divisions interconnected by white matter tracts forming integrated functional networks.
core_concept.explanation: The human brain, weighing ~1400 grams and containing ~86 billion neurons, is the most complex biological structure known. It is organized into four major anatomical and functional divisions. **Brainstem** (midbrain, pons, medulla oblongata): the most primitive region, controlling autonomic functions (respiration—medullary respiratory centers; cardiovascular—medullary vasomotor center; consciousness—reticular activating system). It contains cranial nerve nuclei (III-XII) and serves as the conduit for all ascending sensory and descending motor pathways. **Cerebellum** ("little brain"): contains ~80% of all brain neurons (especially granule cells). It coordinates movement timing, precision, and motor learning via circuits with the cerebral cortex and brainstem; also contributes to cognitive and emotional processing. **Diencephalon**: Thalamus (relay station for all sensory information except olfaction, gating and processing signals to cortex) and hypothalamus (master regulator of homeostasis—temperature, hunger, thirst, circadian rhythms, endocrine control via pituitary, autonomic nervous system integration). **Cerebrum**: The largest division, consisting of two cerebral hemispheres connected by the corpus callosum. The cerebral cortex (gray matter, 2-4 mm thick, folded into gyri and sulci) is divided into four lobes: frontal (executive function, motor planning, speech production—Broca's area), parietal (somatosensory processing, spatial awareness, language comprehension—Wernicke's area), temporal (auditory processing, memory—hippocampus, language comprehension), occipital (visual processing). Subcortical structures include the basal ganglia (motor control, habit formation) and limbic system (emotion, memory, motivation—amygdala, hippocampus, cingulate cortex, hypothalamus). Protective structures: meninges (dura, arachnoid, pia), ventricles (four interconnected cavities containing cerebrospinal fluid, CSF), and the blood-brain barrier. Vascular supply: anterior circulation (internal carotid arteries—anterior and middle cerebral arteries) supplies most of cerebral hemispheres; posterior circulation (vertebral and basilar arteries—posterior cerebral artery) supplies brainstem, cerebellum, occipital lobe. Understanding brain structure is essential for clinical neurology: stroke syndromes (anterior vs. posterior circulation deficits), movement disorders (basal ganglia—Parkinson's, Huntington's; cerebellum—ataxia), memory disorders (hippocampus—Alzheimer's), and consciousness disorders (brainstem—coma, brain death).

image.alt: Lateral view of brain showing four major divisions: cerebrum (frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital lobes), cerebellum, brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla). Sagittal view showing corpus callosum, thalamus, hypothalamus, limbic system structures.
image.caption: Brain Structure: Cerebrum (higher functions), Cerebellum (coordination), Brainstem (vital functions), Limbic System (emotion, memory). These divisions form integrated networks controlling behavior, movement, and homeostasis.

sections.section_title: Introduction: Hierarchical Organization of the Human Brain
sections.introduction: The human brain is the product of over 500 million years of vertebrate evolution, with structures added in layers: the brainstem (reptilian brain) controls basic survival functions; the diencephalon and basal ganglia (paleomammalian) add homeostasis and motor control; the limbic system (early mammalian) enables emotion and memory; and the cerebral cortex (neomammalian) provides language, reasoning, and abstract thought. This hierarchical organization is reflected in both development (neural tube → forebrain, midbrain, hindbrain) and function (brainstem supports cortex; cortex modulates brainstem). The adult brain is protected by the skull, meninges, and CSF cushion; it consumes 20% of the body's oxygen and glucose despite representing only 2% of body weight. Blood supply is provided by two paired arteries: internal carotids (anterior circulation) and vertebral arteries (posterior circulation), which anastomose at the circle of Willis. Clinical localization—determining which brain region is damaged based on neurological deficits—is a core skill in neurology. For example, a patient with right-sided weakness and expressive aphasia has a left frontal stroke (Broca's area, motor cortex); a patient with ataxia and intention tremor has cerebellar pathology; a patient with coma and pinpoint pupils has brainstem involvement. Understanding brain structure—from macroscopic lobes to microscopic cytoarchitecture (Brodmann areas)—is foundational for interpreting neuroimaging (CT, MRI), localizing lesions, and planning neurosurgical approaches.

introduction_image.alt: Evolutionary and developmental hierarchy: neural tube → forebrain (cerebrum, thalamus, hypothalamus), midbrain, hindbrain (pons, medulla, cerebellum). Overlay showing functional layers: brainstem (autonomic), limbic (emotion), cortex (cognition).
introduction_image.caption: Hierarchical Brain Organization: Brainstem (survival), limbic system (emotion, memory), cerebral cortex (cognition). This evolutionary layering is reflected in development and function, with higher centers modulating lower centers.

key_concept.definition: The brain is organized into four major divisions: cerebrum (higher cognition, voluntary movement, sensory processing), cerebellum (motor coordination, balance, motor learning), brainstem (autonomic regulation, cranial nerve functions, arousal), and limbic system (emotion, memory, motivation)—all interconnected by white matter tracts and functioning as integrated networks.
key_concept.context: This anatomical division reflects both evolutionary history and functional specialization. The brainstem is the most primitive, controlling essential life-sustaining functions; damage is often rapidly fatal. The cerebellum fine-tunes movement but is not essential for life; damage causes ataxia but not paralysis. The cerebrum enables the complex behaviors that define human cognition; damage causes specific deficits (aphasia, neglect, apraxia) depending on location. The limbic system bridges emotion and memory, connecting cortical processing to autonomic/endocrine responses. Clinical relevance: Brainstem stroke (locked-in syndrome, coma), cerebellar stroke (ataxia, dysmetria, nystagmus), hemispheric stroke (hemiparesis, aphasia, neglect), limbic system pathology (Alzheimer's—hippocampal atrophy, depression—amygdala dysfunction).

image.alt: Color-coded brain: cerebrum (blue), cerebellum (green), brainstem (red), limbic system (purple). Icons indicate functions: cognition, movement, vital functions, emotion/memory.
image.caption: Functional Divisions: Cerebrum (cognition, voluntary movement); Cerebellum (coordination, motor learning); Brainstem (autonomic, cranial nerves, arousal); Limbic System (emotion, memory, motivation).

sections.key_concept: Each major brain division exhibits specialized structure-function relationships; cerebral cortex is organized into lobes with distinct functions; subcortical structures (basal ganglia, thalamus, hypothalamus) modulate cortical activity and maintain homeostasis.

classifications.type: Cerebrum: Cortical Lobes and Functional Areas
classifications.value: Best for understanding the organization of higher cognitive functions, motor control, sensory processing, and language.
characteristics: Frontal Lobe: Anterior to central sulcus. Precentral gyrus (primary motor cortex, area 4)—motor homunculus, contralateral voluntary movement. Premotor cortex (area 6)—motor planning. Supplementary motor area—complex movement sequences. Prefrontal cortex (areas 9-12, 46)—executive function (planning, decision-making, working memory, personality). Broca's area (areas 44, 45, left hemisphere)—speech production (expressive language). Frontal eye fields (area 8)—voluntary eye movements.

Parietal Lobe: Posterior to central sulcus. Postcentral gyrus (primary somatosensory cortex, areas 3,1,2)—sensory homunculus, contralateral touch, proprioception, pain, temperature. Superior parietal lobule—spatial orientation, attention. Inferior parietal lobule (supramarginal gyrus area 40, angular gyrus area 39)—language processing, reading, arithmetic. Wernicke's area (area 22, left posterior temporal extending into parietal)—language comprehension.

Temporal Lobe: Inferior to lateral sulcus. Primary auditory cortex (areas 41, 42, Heschl's gyrus)—sound processing. Wernicke's area (posterior temporal)—language comprehension. Hippocampus (medial temporal)—declarative memory formation. Amygdala (anterior medial temporal)—emotion processing (fear, aggression). Fusiform gyrus (area 37)—face recognition (prosopagnosia with damage).

Occipital Lobe: Posterior. Primary visual cortex (V1, area 17, striate cortex)—retinotopic map, basic visual processing. Visual association areas (V2-V5, areas 18, 19)—motion (V5/MT), color (V4), object recognition (inferotemporal cortex).
Insula (Insular Cortex): Deep within lateral sulcus. Interoception (visceral sensation), empathy, addiction, pain processing.
classifications.behavior: Cortical organization follows several principles: **Contralaterality**: Motor and sensory cortices control opposite side of body (decussation in medulla). **Topographic maps**: Motor and sensory homunculi represent body parts proportionally (large areas for face, hands). **Functional specialization**: Specific regions perform specific functions (e.g., Broca's area for speech production, fusiform gyrus for face recognition). **Lateralization**: Left hemisphere typically dominant for language (90% of right-handed individuals, 70% of left-handed), analytical processing; right hemisphere for visuospatial attention, emotional prosody, holistic processing. **Hierarchical processing**: Sensory information flows from primary cortex (simple features) to association cortex (complex integration). **Plasticity**: Cortical maps reorganize after injury or learning. Clinical correlations: **Frontal lobe lesions**: Contralateral weakness (motor cortex), personality changes (prefrontal—disinhibition, apathy), Broca's aphasia (non-fluent, telegraphic speech, preserved comprehension), frontal release signs (grasp, suck reflexes). **Parietal lobe lesions**: Contralateral sensory loss, neglect (right parietal—left hemispatial neglect), Gerstmann syndrome (left angular gyrus—agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, left-right confusion). **Temporal lobe lesions**: Memory impairment (hippocampus), Wernicke's aphasia (fluent, nonsense speech, poor comprehension), seizures (temporal lobe epilepsy—aura, automatisms). **Occipital lobe lesions**: Contralateral homonymous hemianopia (visual field cut), cortical blindness (bilateral occipital).

examples.process: Primary Motor and Somatosensory Cortices: Homunculus Organization
examples.explanation: The motor and sensory homunculi are topographic maps of the body: (1) **Motor homunculus** (precentral gyrus): arranged medial to lateral—leg (medial), trunk, arm, hand, face (lateral). Proportion of cortex dedicated to a body part correlates with motor control precision (large area for hands, lips, tongue). (2) **Sensory homunculus** (postcentral gyrus): similar organization; hand and face disproportionately large. (3) **Clinical localization**: Stroke affecting medial frontal cortex → contralateral leg weakness (paracentral lobule); lateral frontal → face and arm weakness. (4) **Penfield's maps**: Discovered during neurosurgery with cortical stimulation; patients reported sensations (sensory) or movements (motor). (5) **Plasticity**: Amputation leads to cortical reorganization—adjacent areas (e.g., face) expand into the hand area (phantom limb sensations). Significance: The homunculus demonstrates the principle of cortical localization and topography, enabling precise lesion localization based on deficit pattern.

image.alt: Motor homunculus on precentral gyrus and sensory homunculus on postcentral gyrus showing distorted body proportions (large hands, face, tongue). Medial to lateral organization.
image.caption: Motor and Sensory Homunculi: Topographic maps of the body in precentral (motor) and postcentral (sensory) gyri. Body parts requiring fine motor control (hands, face) or high sensory acuity (lips, fingers) occupy disproportionately large cortical areas.

examples.process: Language Areas: Broca's and Wernicke's Aphasias
examples.explanation: Language is localized to the left hemisphere in most individuals (dominant hemisphere). (1) **Broca's area** (inferior frontal gyrus, areas 44, 45): Motor programming of speech. Lesion → Broca's aphasia (non-fluent, agrammatic, telegraphic speech; comprehension relatively preserved; frustration aware). (2) **Wernicke's area** (superior temporal gyrus, area 22): Language comprehension. Lesion → Wernicke's aphasia (fluent but meaningless speech (word salad), poor comprehension, anosognosia—unaware of deficit). (3) **Arcuate fasciculus**: White matter tract connecting Broca's and Wernicke's areas. Lesion → conduction aphasia (fluent, intact comprehension, impaired repetition). (4) **Global aphasia**: Large perisylvian lesion affecting both Broca's and Wernicke's areas → severe impairment in production and comprehension. (5) **Right hemisphere contributions**: Prosody (emotional tone), discourse-level processing. Significance: Language localization exemplifies functional specialization and the importance of white matter connections. Aphasia patterns enable precise lesion localization in stroke patients.

image.alt: Lateral view of left hemisphere showing Broca's area (frontal), Wernicke's area (temporal), arcuate fasciculus connecting them. CT scans showing lesions; speech examples for each aphasia type.
image.caption: Language Areas: Broca's area (speech production) and Wernicke's area (language comprehension) are connected by the arcuate fasciculus. Lesions cause distinct aphasia syndromes: Broca's (non-fluent), Wernicke's (fluent nonsense), conduction (impaired repetition).

classifications.value: Best for understanding movement disorders (Parkinson's, Huntington's, dystonia) and the role of subcortical structures in motor control.
characteristics: Components: Caudate nucleus, putamen (together striatum), globus pallidus (internal GPi, external GPe), subthalamic nucleus (STN), substantia nigra (pars compacta SNc, pars reticulata SNr).
Direct Pathway: Cortex → striatum (D1 receptors) → GPi/SNr (inhibition) → thalamus (disinhibition) → cortex → facilitates movement. Net effect: promotes movement.

Indirect Pathway: Cortex → striatum (D2 receptors) → GPe (inhibition) → STN (disinhibition) → GPi/SNr (excitation) → thalamus (inhibition) → cortex → suppresses movement. Net effect: inhibits movement.

Dopamine Modulation: SNc dopamine activates D1 (direct, excitatory) and inhibits D2 (indirect, inhibitory). Dopamine loss (Parkinson's) → reduced direct pathway, enhanced indirect pathway → bradykinesia, rigidity.
Hyperdirect Pathway: Cortex → STN directly → GPi/SNr → rapid inhibition of thalamus → stop signal.
classifications.behavior: The basal ganglia form parallel loops (motor, oculomotor, associative, limbic) that modulate cortical activity. The **motor loop** selects and initiates voluntary movements. Dopamine from SNc balances direct and indirect pathways. **Parkinson's disease**: Degeneration of SNc dopamine neurons → imbalance (direct ↓, indirect ↑) → bradykinesia (slowness), rigidity (cogwheel), resting tremor (4-6 Hz), postural instability. Treatment: L-DOPA (dopamine precursor), dopamine agonists, deep brain stimulation (STN or GPi). **Huntington's disease**: Degeneration of striatal GABAergic neurons (indirect pathway preferentially affected early) → chorea (involuntary dance-like movements). **Hemiballismus**: STN lesion (often stroke) → disinhibition of thalamus → wild, flailing movements of contralateral limbs. **Dystonia**: Sustained muscle contractions, twisting postures; pathophysiology involves abnormal basal ganglia-thalamocortical signaling. Significance: The basal ganglia are not direct motor output pathways but rather gatekeepers that select and refine movements. Understanding their circuitry explains movement disorders and guides therapeutic interventions (pharmacological, surgical).

examples.process: Parkinson's Disease: Dopamine Depletion and Circuit Dysfunction
examples.explanation: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a prototypical basal ganglia disorder: (1) Pathology: Loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc), with Lewy bodies (α-synuclein aggregates). (2) Circuit consequences: Dopamine loss reduces activation of D1 receptors (direct pathway) and reduces inhibition of D2 receptors (indirect pathway) → direct pathway hypoactive, indirect pathway hyperactive → excessive GPi/SNr output → excessive inhibition of thalamus → reduced cortical activation. (3) Clinical: Bradykinesia (difficulty initiating movement, reduced arm swing, hypophonia), rigidity (cogwheel, lead-pipe), resting tremor (pill-rolling), postural instability (late). (4) Non-motor: anosmia, depression, REM sleep behavior disorder, autonomic dysfunction, cognitive impairment. (5) Treatment: L-DOPA (converted to dopamine in remaining neurons) restores dopaminergic tone; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline); COMT inhibitors (entacapone); deep brain stimulation of STN or GPi (reduces abnormal basal ganglia output). (6) Advanced: motor fluctuations, dyskinesias (L-DOPA-induced). Significance: Parkinson's disease illustrates how degeneration of a specific neuronal population (SNc dopamine neurons) causes predictable circuit dysfunction and clinical syndrome.

image.alt: Basal ganglia circuitry in normal and Parkinson's disease: direct pathway (blue), indirect pathway (red); dopamine loss reduces direct activation and increases indirect activity; clinical features of Parkinson's.
image.caption: Parkinson's Disease Circuit: Dopamine loss from substantia nigra reduces direct pathway (D1) activation and increases indirect pathway (D2) activity, leading to excessive GPi/SNr output and bradykinesia/rigidity.

classifications.type: Brainstem: Cranial Nerves and Vital Centers
classifications.value: Best for understanding brainstem stroke syndromes, cranial nerve deficits, and autonomic regulation.
characteristics: Midbrain (Mesencephalon): Superior colliculi (visual reflexes), inferior colliculi (auditory reflexes), periaqueductal gray (pain modulation), oculomotor (CN III) and trochlear (CN IV) nuclei, cerebral peduncles (corticospinal tracts), substantia nigra, red nucleus.

Pons: Pontine nuclei (relay between cortex and cerebellum), trigeminal (CN V), abducens (CN VI), facial (CN VII), vestibulocochlear (CN VIII) nuclei; corticospinal tracts (pontine fibers), medial lemniscus (sensory), paramedian pontine reticular formation (horizontal gaze).

Medulla Oblongata: Pyramids (corticospinal tracts, decussation), inferior olivary nuclei, gracile/cuneate nuclei (sensory relay), hypoglossal (CN XII), vagus (CN X), glossopharyngeal (CN IX), accessory (CN XI) nuclei; respiratory centers (dorsal and ventral respiratory groups), cardiovascular centers (vasomotor, cardiac), chemoreceptor trigger zone.
classifications.behavior: The brainstem has three fundamental functions: (1) **Conduit**: All ascending sensory tracts (spinothalamic, dorsal columns) and descending motor tracts (corticospinal, corticobulbar) pass through brainstem. (2) **Cranial nerve functions**: Contains nuclei for CN III-XII; mediates pupillary light reflex (CN II afferent, CN III efferent), corneal reflex (CN V afferent, CN VII efferent), gag reflex (CN IX afferent, CN X efferent), and eye movements (CN III, IV, VI). (3) **Autonomic regulation**: Medullary centers control respiration (inspiratory ramp, expiratory off-switch), heart rate (vagal tone), blood pressure (baroreceptor reflex), swallowing, coughing, vomiting. **Reticular activating system (RAS)** : Network of nuclei in tegmentum maintains arousal and consciousness; damage causes coma. **Brainstem reflexes** are critical for neurological examination: pupillary light reflex (tests CN II, III, midbrain), corneal reflex (CN V, VII, pons), oculocephalic reflex (doll's eyes, tests brainstem gaze centers), gag reflex (CN IX, X, medulla). **Brainstem stroke syndromes** are classically described (e.g., Weber's syndrome—CN III palsy + contralateral hemiparesis, midbrain; Millard-Gubler—CN VII palsy + contralateral hemiparesis, pons; Wallenberg's (lateral medullary)—ipsilateral ataxia, Horner's, facial numbness + contralateral pain/temp loss). Significance: Brainstem lesions are often life-threatening due to involvement of vital centers; localization requires careful assessment of cranial nerve deficits, motor/sensory patterns, and level of consciousness.

examples.explanation: Wallenberg syndrome is a classic brainstem stroke syndrome from occlusion of the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or vertebral artery, affecting lateral medulla. (1) Structures affected: Inferior cerebellar peduncle (ipsilateral ataxia), vestibular nuclei (vertigo, nystagmus), spinothalamic tract (contralateral pain/temp loss), descending sympathetic fibers (ipsilateral Horner's—ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), nucleus ambiguus (ipsilateral dysphagia, dysphonia), spinal trigeminal nucleus (ipsilateral facial pain/temp loss). (2) Clinical presentation: Acute onset of vertigo, dysphagia, hoarseness, ipsilateral ataxia, Horner's, facial numbness, contralateral body pain/temp loss (alternating sensory syndrome). (3) Diagnosis: MRI brain shows lateral medullary infarction. (4) Prognosis: Variable; dysphagia may require feeding tube; recovery often substantial. Significance: Wallenberg syndrome illustrates the compact organization of brainstem structures—a small lesion affects multiple systems, producing a characteristic syndrome that localizes precisely to lateral medulla.

image.alt: Cross-section of medulla showing structures in lateral medulla (inferior cerebellar peduncle, spinothalamic tract, descending sympathetic fibers, nucleus ambiguus, spinal trigeminal nucleus). Clinical features list. MRI showing lateral medullary infarct.
image.caption: Wallenberg Syndrome (Lateral Medullary): PICA/vertebral artery occlusion affects lateral medulla, causing ipsilateral ataxia, Horner's, facial numbness, dysphagia + contralateral pain/temp loss.

classifications.type: Limbic System: Emotion, Memory, and Motivation
classifications.value: Best for understanding memory disorders (Alzheimer's, Korsakoff's), emotional processing (anxiety, depression, fear), and motivation (reward, addiction).
characteristics: Hippocampus: Medial temporal lobe; critical for declarative memory formation (episodic, spatial). Pyramidal neurons in CA1, CA3 regions; entorhinal cortex input via perforant path; output via fornix. Bilateral damage causes anterograde amnesia (inability to form new memories) with preserved remote memory, procedural memory.

Amygdala: Anterior medial temporal; processes emotion, particularly fear and threat detection. Inputs from sensory systems; outputs to hypothalamus (autonomic response), brainstem (startle), cortex (emotional awareness). Damage causes Kluver-Bucy syndrome (hyperorality, hypersexuality, placidity) or impaired fear recognition.
Cingulate Cortex: Anterior cingulate (emotion, attention, error detection); posterior cingulate (memory, default mode network).

Hypothalamus: Master regulator of homeostasis; connects limbic system to autonomic and endocrine systems via pituitary. Controls hunger, thirst, temperature, circadian rhythms, sexual behavior, stress response (HPA axis).
Nucleus Accumbens: Ventral striatum; reward, pleasure, addiction (mesolimbic dopamine pathway).
Fornix, Mammillary Bodies, Anterior Thalamus: Components of Papez circuit (memory).
classifications.behavior: The limbic system integrates emotional states with memory and motivation, linking cortical processing to autonomic/endocrine responses. **Hippocampus** is essential for converting short-term to long-term declarative memories; damage (Alzheimer's, anoxia, herpes encephalitis) causes profound anterograde amnesia (patient HM). **Amygdala** processes fear conditioning; damage impairs fear recognition and emotional memory. **Papez circuit** (hippocampus → fornix → mammillary bodies → anterior thalamus → cingulate cortex → hippocampus) is critical for memory; disruption in Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome (thiamine deficiency, alcoholism) causes amnesia with confabulation. **Hypothalamus** coordinates autonomic, endocrine, and behavioral responses to emotional stimuli: sympathetic activation (fight-or-flight), parasympathetic (rest-and-digest), HPA axis (stress—cortisol). Clinical correlations: **Alzheimer's disease**: Early hippocampal atrophy → memory impairment; spreads to cortex. **Temporal lobe epilepsy**: Seizures often originate in hippocampus/amygdala → aura (déjà vu, fear), automatisms. **Depression**: Amygdala hyperreactivity, prefrontal hypofunction; antidepressants modulate limbic circuits. **PTSD**: Hyperactive amygdala, impaired prefrontal regulation of fear. **Kluver-Bucy syndrome**: Bilateral temporal lobe lesions (including amygdala) → docility, hyperorality, hypersexuality, visual agnosia. Significance: The limbic system bridges cognition and emotion, explaining how memories acquire emotional valence and how emotional states influence autonomic function.

examples.process: Patient HM: Hippocampus and Declarative Memory
examples.explanation: Patient HM (Henry Molaison) is the most famous case in memory research: (1) History: Bilateral medial temporal lobe resection (including hippocampus) for intractable epilepsy (age 27). (2) Outcome: Severe anterograde amnesia—unable to form new declarative (explicit) memories; could not recognize hospital staff, read same magazine repeatedly. (3) Preserved functions: Procedural memory (learned mirror tracing task with practice, but no recollection of practicing), short-term memory (normal digit span), remote memories (childhood memories intact), intelligence, language. (4) Impaired functions: Declarative memory (facts, events) cannot be consolidated into long-term storage. (5) Implications: Demonstrated that hippocampus is critical for converting short-term to long-term declarative memory, but not for memory storage itself (distributed in cortex) nor for procedural memory (basal ganglia, cerebellum). (6) Neuropathology: Post-mortem showed hippocampal and entorhinal cortex loss. Significance: HM established the fundamental dissociation between declarative (hippocampal-dependent) and procedural (non-hippocampal) memory systems, revolutionizing understanding of memory organization.

image.alt: MRI of HM showing bilateral medial temporal lobe resection; diagram of memory systems (declarative vs. procedural); timeline showing spared and impaired functions.
image.caption: Patient HM: Bilateral hippocampal resection caused severe anterograde amnesia (unable to form new declarative memories) but spared procedural memory, demonstrating the hippocampus's role in declarative memory consolidation.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of Brain Structure and Function

principles.principle: Hierarchical Organization: Brainstem Supports Higher Centers
principles.statement: The brain is organized hierarchically: brainstem and hypothalamus regulate basic survival functions (respiration, cardiovascular, homeostasis); diencephalon and basal ganglia modulate sensory and motor processing; limbic system integrates emotion with memory and autonomic responses; cerebral cortex provides executive control, language, and complex cognition—with higher centers exerting modulatory control over lower centers.
principles.application: Functional inference: (1) Brainstem autonomic centers operate without cortical input (decorticate animals maintain respiration, blood pressure). (2) Cortex modulates these centers via corticobulbar and corticoreticular pathways. (3) Loss of cortical input (e.g., stroke) may release brainstem reflexes (decorticate/decerebrate posturing). (4) Severe brainstem damage abolishes consciousness (coma, brain death) even with intact cortex. Clinical application: Determining level of brain dysfunction: metabolic encephalopathy affects cortex diffusely (confusion, delirium); brainstem stroke causes focal cranial nerve deficits and may impair consciousness; herniation syndromes compress brainstem, causing progressive loss of consciousness and autonomic instability.
principles.example: Brain death determination: (1) Brain death = irreversible loss of all brain functions (cortex and brainstem). (2) Prerequisites: known irreversible cause, absence of confounding factors (hypothermia, drug intoxication). (3) Examination: Coma (no response to any stimulus), absence of brainstem reflexes (pupillary light reflex, corneal reflex, oculocephalic reflex, oculovestibular reflex, gag reflex, cough reflex), apnea (no respiratory effort despite CO₂ elevation). (4) Confirmatory tests: EEG (electrocerebral silence), cerebral blood flow studies (no flow). (5) Significance: Brain death is legally and medically equivalent to death; organ donation possible. The hierarchical organization means that brainstem function is necessary for consciousness and survival; cortical loss alone does not equal death (persistent vegetative state).
principles.analogy: Hierarchical brain organization is like a corporation: Brainstem = building operations (electricity, plumbing)—essential for function; Limbic system = middle management (emotion, motivation)—coordinates responses; Cortex = CEO (strategy, language, complex decisions)—directs overall behavior. The CEO can override middle management, but if operations fail, the entire organization shuts down.

image.alt: Pyramid diagram: brainstem (survival), diencephalon (homeostasis), limbic (emotion/memory), basal ganglia (motor), cortex (cognition). Arrows showing bidirectional modulation.
image.caption: Hierarchical Organization: Brainstem (survival) → Diencephalon (homeostasis) → Limbic System (emotion/memory) → Basal Ganglia (motor) → Cerebral Cortex (cognition). Higher centers modulate lower centers; lower centers support higher centers.

principles.statement: The brain maintains topographic maps of the body (somatotopy), visual field (retinotopy), auditory frequency (tonotopy), and movement space (motor maps)—enabling precise localization of function and lesion effects.
principles.application: Anatomical inference: (1) Motor homunculus: medial (leg) to lateral (face) in precentral gyrus; (2) Sensory homunculus: similar organization in postcentral gyrus; (3) Visual cortex: retinotopic map—macula at occipital pole, peripheral vision more anterior; (4) Auditory cortex: tonotopic map—high frequencies medially, low frequencies laterally; (5) Clinical: Stroke location predicts deficit pattern—medial frontal → contralateral leg weakness; occipital pole lesion → central (macular) visual field cut; lateral occipital → peripheral field cut. Significance: Topographic organization enables precise lesion localization based on deficit patterns, guiding acute stroke management and rehabilitation planning.
principles.example: Visual field defects from occipital lesions: (1) Primary visual cortex (V1, area 17) has retinotopic organization: contralateral visual field represented. (2) Macular representation at occipital pole; peripheral representation more anterior. (3) Lesion of left occipital cortex → right homonymous hemianopia (loss of right visual field in both eyes). (4) Lesion of occipital pole (macular sparing) → peripheral field loss with central vision preserved. (5) Bilateral occipital lesions → cortical blindness (patient unaware of blindness—Anton's syndrome). Significance: Visual field testing localizes lesions along the visual pathway (optic nerve, chiasm, tract, radiation, cortex). Topographic organization allows precise mapping of deficits to anatomical location.
principles.analogy: Topographic maps are like a city map: The motor cortex is a map of the body's muscles (like a city map showing streets); the sensory cortex maps touch (like a map showing landmarks). Damage to a specific street (cortical area) causes predictable deficits (losing that street's function). The map's layout (medial leg, lateral face) allows you to locate the damage by observing which functions are lost.

image.alt: Motor and sensory homunculi; retinotopic map in visual cortex; tonotopic map in auditory cortex. Lesion examples showing deficit patterns.
image.caption: Topographic Organization: The brain maintains maps of the body (motor/sensory homunculi), visual field (retinotopy), and sound frequency (tonotopy). Lesion location predicts deficit pattern.

principles.statement: The cerebral hemispheres are specialized: the left hemisphere is typically dominant for language, analytical reasoning, and sequential processing; the right hemisphere is specialized for visuospatial attention, emotional prosody, and holistic processing—with the corpus callosum enabling interhemispheric communication.
principles.application: Functional inference: (1) Left hemisphere lesions: aphasia (Broca's, Wernicke's, conduction), apraxia, right hemiparesis/hemisensory loss. (2) Right hemisphere lesions: left hemispatial neglect, prosopagnosia (face recognition), impaired emotional prosody, left hemiparesis/hemisensory loss. (3) Corpus callosum lesions: disconnection syndromes (left hand unable to name objects held in left hand—anomia, left hand apraxia). Clinical application: Stroke localization—left hemisphere stroke presents with aphasia and right-sided weakness; right hemisphere stroke presents with neglect and left-sided weakness. Wada test (intracarotid amobarbital) determines language dominance pre-surgically. Significance: Lateralization explains why similar-sized strokes in left vs. right hemisphere cause different clinical syndromes; it is critical for surgical planning (avoiding damage to dominant hemisphere language areas).
principles.example: Left hemispatial neglect from right parietal stroke: (1) Right inferior parietal lobule (especially angular gyrus) is critical for attention to both sides of space. (2) Lesion → patient fails to attend to left side of body and environment. (3) Clinical: Patient bumps into left doorframe, leaves left arm hanging (doesn't use), ignores food on left side of plate, bisects lines far to the right, may deny left limbs are theirs (asomatognosia). (4) Not a primary sensory or motor deficit—patient can feel touch if attention directed. (5) Significance: Neglect is more common and severe with right hemisphere lesions than left, demonstrating hemispheric specialization for spatial attention. Recovery is variable; prism adaptation may help. Significance: Lateralization has profound clinical implications—right hemisphere strokes are often under-recognized because neglect is mistaken for indifference or dementia.
principles.analogy: Hemispheric specialization is like two business partners: Left hemisphere = analytical partner (language, math, details). Right hemisphere = creative partner (spatial awareness, emotion, big picture). They communicate via a dedicated phone line (corpus callosum). Damage to the left partner causes specific language/analysis deficits; damage to the right partner causes spatial/emotional deficits.

image.alt: Left hemisphere labeled with language areas (Broca's, Wernicke's), analytical functions; right hemisphere labeled with spatial attention, prosody, face recognition. Neglect drawing test (clock, flower) showing left-sided omissions.
image.caption: Hemispheric Lateralization: Left hemisphere—language, analytical processing; Right hemisphere—spatial attention, emotional prosody, holistic processing. Right parietal stroke causes left hemispatial neglect (clock drawing omits left side).

components.definition: Cerebrum (2 hemispheres, 4 lobes, basal ganglia, limbic structures); Diencephalon (thalamus, hypothalamus); Brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla); Cerebellum (hemispheres, vermis); Ventricular system (4 ventricles, CSF); Meninges (dura, arachnoid, pia); Vascular supply (circle of Willis, anterior/middle/posterior cerebral arteries, vertebrobasilar system).
components.equation: $\text{Brain weight} \approx 2\% \text{ of body weight}, \text{ consumes } 20\% \text{ of } O_2 \text{ and glucose}$
components.interpretation: The brain's disproportionate metabolic demand (20% of total body despite 2% mass) reflects its high energy requirements for maintaining ion gradients (Na+/K+ ATPase), neurotransmitter cycling, and signaling. Cerebral blood flow is tightly regulated (autoregulation) to maintain constant perfusion across blood pressure fluctuations; disruption causes ischemia or hemorrhage.

image.alt: Sagittal MRI labeled with cerebrum, corpus callosum, thalamus, hypothalamus, brainstem, cerebellum, pituitary gland, ventricles. Circle of Willis anatomy.
image.caption: Key Components: Cerebrum (higher functions), Diencephalon (thalamus, hypothalamus), Brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla), Cerebellum (coordination). Vascular supply: anterior and posterior circulations via circle of Willis.

categories.description: The ventricular system comprises four interconnected cavities (lateral ventricles, third ventricle, cerebral aqueduct, fourth ventricle) containing cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), which provides buoyancy, shock absorption, waste clearance, and chemical stability.
categories.detailed_mechanism: CSF production: Choroid plexus in lateral, third, and fourth ventricles secretes ~500 mL/day (total volume ~150 mL). Circulation: Lateral ventricles → interventricular foramina (Monro) → third ventricle → cerebral aqueduct (Sylvius) → fourth ventricle → median (Magendie) and lateral (Luschka) apertures → subarachnoid space → arachnoid granulations → superior sagittal sinus → venous system. Functions: Buoyancy (reduces brain effective weight from 1400g to 50g), mechanical protection (shock absorption), waste clearance (glymphatic system), ionic homeostasis. Clinical: Hydrocephalus—excess CSF; non-communicating (obstruction within ventricles—tumor, aqueductal stenosis); communicating (impaired absorption—subarachnoid hemorrhage, meningitis). Normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH): triad of gait disturbance, cognitive decline, urinary incontinence; treatable with ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt. Lumbar puncture: CSF analysis for meningitis (pleocytosis, low glucose), subarachnoid hemorrhage (xanthochromia), multiple sclerosis (oligoclonal bands).

examples.explanation: Hydrocephalus is CSF accumulation causing ventricular enlargement: (1) Non-communicating hydrocephalus: Obstruction within ventricles (e.g., tumor compressing cerebral aqueduct, intraventricular hemorrhage). (2) Communicating hydrocephalus: Impaired CSF absorption (e.g., post-subarachnoid hemorrhage, post-meningitis) or overproduction (choroid plexus papilloma). (3) Clinical: Infants—macrocephaly, bulging fontanelle, sunsetting eyes (upward gaze palsy), irritability; Adults—headache, nausea, papilledema, cognitive decline, gait impairment. (4) Normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH): Communicating hydrocephalus with normal CSF pressure; gait apraxia (magnetic gait), cognitive decline (subcortical), urinary incontinence; high-volume lumbar puncture may temporarily improve gait; VP shunt is definitive treatment. (5) Imaging: CT/MRI shows ventriculomegaly (Evan's ratio >0.3), periventricular edema. Significance: CSF circulation is essential for brain health; obstruction causes potentially reversible neurological deficits.

image.alt: Ventricular system with obstruction sites; CT showing ventriculomegaly in hydrocephalus; VP shunt diagram; clinical features of NPH (gait, cognition, incontinence).
image.caption: Hydrocephalus: CSF accumulation causes ventriculomegaly. Non-communicating (obstruction) vs. communicating (impaired absorption). Normal pressure hydrocephalus presents with gait apraxia, cognitive decline, urinary incontinence—treatable with VP shunt.

categories.type: Vascular Supply: Arterial Territories and Stroke Syndromes
categories.description: The brain is supplied by anterior (internal carotid) and posterior (vertebrobasilar) circulations, which anastomose at the circle of Willis. Each major artery supplies specific brain regions; occlusion causes characteristic stroke syndromes.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Anterior circulation: Internal carotid artery → anterior cerebral artery (ACA) and middle cerebral artery (MCA). ACA supplies medial frontal/parietal (leg motor/sensory cortex); occlusion → contralateral leg weakness. MCA supplies lateral frontal/parietal/temporal (face/arm motor/sensory, language areas); occlusion → contralateral face/arm weakness, aphasia (left), neglect (right). Posterior circulation: Vertebral arteries → basilar artery → posterior cerebral artery (PCA). Vertebrobasilar system supplies brainstem, cerebellum, occipital lobe, thalamus. PCA occlusion → contralateral homonymous hemianopia (occipital), thalamic stroke (sensory loss, thalamic pain). Circle of Willis: Collateral pathway; incomplete in ~50% (absent posterior communicating arteries).

examples.explanation: MCA is the most commonly affected vessel in ischemic stroke (70-80% of strokes). (1) Anatomy: MCA supplies lateral cerebral hemispheres—frontal (motor cortex for face/arm, Broca's area), parietal (sensory cortex, Wernicke's area), temporal, insula. (2) Superior division MCA: Frontal lobe involvement—contralateral face/arm weakness (leg spared), Broca's aphasia (if dominant hemisphere). (3) Inferior division MCA: Temporal/parietal involvement—Wernicke's aphasia (dominant), contralateral hemisensory loss, neglect (non-dominant). (4) Complete MCA: Large territory—hemiparesis (face/arm > leg), hemisensory loss, aphasia or neglect, gaze preference toward lesion (frontal eye fields). (5) Imaging: CT/CTA shows MCA hyperdensity (clot), ASPECTS score for early ischemic changes. (6) Treatment: IV tPA within 4.5 hours; mechanical thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion (up to 24 hours). Significance: MCA stroke syndromes are the most common; recognizing the pattern (face/arm weakness, aphasia/neglect) enables rapid diagnosis and treatment.

image.alt: MCA territory on brain surface; clinical deficits: contralateral face/arm weakness, aphasia (dominant), neglect (non-dominant); CT showing MCA infarct; thrombectomy device.
image.caption: MCA Stroke: MCA supplies lateral hemisphere (face/arm motor/sensory, language areas). Superior division → face/arm weakness + Broca's aphasia; Inferior division → Wernicke's aphasia + sensory loss; Complete MCA → hemiparesis, aphasia/neglect.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Localizing Brain Lesions from Clinical Syndromes

worked_examples.title: Localizing Brain Lesions from Clinical Syndromes
worked_examples.problem: A 72-year-old man presents with acute onset of right-sided weakness (face and arm > leg), expressive aphasia (non-fluent, telegraphic speech, intact comprehension), and right-sided sensory loss. (1) What artery is likely occluded? (2) What specific cortical areas are involved? (3) What is the clinical syndrome? (4) What is the acute management?

step_by_step.calculation: Right-sided weakness (face/arm > leg) → left hemisphere motor cortex; expressive aphasia → Broca's area (left inferior frontal gyrus); sensory loss → left primary somatosensory cortex. Face/arm involvement with leg sparing suggests lateral convexity (MCA territory) rather than medial (ACA). Pattern localizes to left middle cerebral artery (MCA) superior division territory.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify specific cortical areas and Brodmann areas.
step_by_step.calculation: Precentral gyrus (primary motor cortex, area 4) → contralateral weakness. Postcentral gyrus (primary somatosensory cortex, areas 3,1,2) → contralateral sensory loss. Broca's area (areas 44,45) → expressive aphasia. These areas are all supplied by superior division MCA. Leg motor/sensory cortex is supplied by ACA (medial frontal/parietal), explaining sparing of leg.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine clinical syndrome and likely pathology.
step_by_step.calculation: Acute onset, focal deficits consistent with vascular territory → acute ischemic stroke. Most common mechanism: thromboembolic (cardioembolic from atrial fibrillation, or carotid plaque). Differential: intracerebral hemorrhage (less common, would show hyperdensity on CT).

step_by_step.explanation: Determine acute management based on time window.
step_by_step.calculation: Immediate: non-contrast CT to exclude hemorrhage. If no hemorrhage and last known well <4.5 hours: IV alteplase (tPA) if no contraindications. CT angiography (CTA) to assess for large vessel occlusion; if present and within 24 hours, mechanical thrombectomy (if salvageable tissue, small infarct core, good collaterals). Admit to stroke unit, aspirin within 48 hours, blood pressure management (permissive hypertension unless tPA given), statin, secondary prevention (anticoagulation if atrial fibrillation, antiplatelet if not).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Occluded artery: Left middle cerebral artery (MCA), superior division. (2) Cortical areas: Primary motor cortex (face/arm), Broca's area (inferior frontal gyrus), primary somatosensory cortex (face/arm). (3) Clinical syndrome: Acute ischemic stroke, left MCA superior division, causing right hemiparesis (face/arm > leg), Broca's aphasia, right hemisensory loss. (4) Acute management: CT to exclude hemorrhage; IV tPA if within 4.5 hours; mechanical thrombectomy if large vessel occlusion; stroke unit admission; secondary prevention.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding brain structure—cortical localization, Brodmann areas, vascular territories—enables precise lesion localization from clinical examination. MCA stroke localization guides acute treatment (thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion) and predicts prognosis (recovery of language, motor function).

sections.introduction: Voluntary movement requires integrated activity of cerebral cortex (planning, initiation), basal ganglia (selection, gating), cerebellum (coordination, timing), brainstem (posture, basic movement), and spinal cord (final output). This flow illustrates hierarchical motor control.

process_flow.description: Prefrontal cortex determines goal and strategy; premotor cortex plans movement sequence.
process_flow.mechanism: Prefrontal cortex (intention) → Supplementary motor area (SMA, sequence planning) → Premotor cortex (motor preparation). Involves basal ganglia (habit selection) and cerebellum (timing prediction).
process_flow.result: Motor plan formed; ready for execution

image.alt: Prefrontal cortex, SMA, premotor cortex activation during motor planning; functional MRI showing planning networks.
image.caption: Step 1: Motor Planning. Prefrontal cortex (goal), SMA (sequence), premotor cortex (preparation) plan the movement before execution.

process_flow.description: Primary motor cortex (M1) generates descending commands to spinal cord.
process_flow.mechanism: M1 pyramidal neurons (Betz cells) generate action potentials; corticospinal tract axons descend through internal capsule, brainstem, decussate in medulla (pyramidal decussation), synapse on spinal motor neurons (directly or via interneurons).
process_flow.result: Descending motor command to spinal cord

image.alt: Primary motor cortex (precentral gyrus) with corticospinal tract descending through internal capsule, brainstem, decussating in medulla, synapsing on spinal motor neurons.
image.caption: Step 2: Motor Execution. Primary motor cortex generates descending commands via corticospinal tract, which decussates in medulla, enabling contralateral movement control.

process_flow.description: Basal ganglia select and initiate movement; cerebellum coordinates timing and precision.
process_flow.mechanism: Basal ganglia: Direct pathway facilitates desired movement; indirect pathway inhibits competing movements. Dopamine from SNc modulates balance. Cerebellum: Receives motor plan from cortex (via pontine nuclei), receives sensory feedback (spinocerebellar tracts); outputs via thalamus to cortex, adjusting timing and force.

image.alt: Basal ganglia (striatum, GP, STN, SNc) and cerebellum circuits; inputs from cortex, outputs via thalamus back to cortex.
image.caption: Step 3: Motor Modulation. Basal ganglia select and gate movements; cerebellum coordinates timing and precision via feedback loops with cortex.

process_flow.description: Spinal motor neurons activate muscles; sensory feedback adjusts ongoing movement.
process_flow.mechanism: Alpha motor neurons in ventral horn → neuromuscular junction → muscle contraction. Muscle spindles and Golgi tendon organs provide proprioceptive feedback via spinal reflex arcs and ascending tracts to cerebellum/cortex.
process_flow.result: Voluntary movement executed; feedback enables adaptation

image.alt: Spinal cord cross-section showing motor neuron to muscle; muscle spindle sensory feedback loop; descending corticospinal tract input.
image.caption: Step 4: Motor Output. Spinal motor neurons activate muscles. Sensory feedback (muscle spindles) adjusts ongoing movement via spinal reflexes and ascending pathways to cerebellum.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Connectomics and Brain Networks
sections.definition: The brain is organized as a network of interconnected regions (nodes) connected by white matter tracts (edges). Modern connectomics maps structural connectivity (diffusion MRI tractography) and functional connectivity (resting-state fMRI), revealing that cognitive functions emerge from distributed network activity rather than isolated regions.

process_types.energy_profile: The DMN is a set of brain regions (medial prefrontal cortex, posterior cingulate, angular gyrus, medial temporal lobes) that is active during rest, mind-wandering, and self-referential thought; deactivates during goal-directed tasks.
process_types.direction: DMN discovery: Resting-state fMRI revealed correlated activity in these regions. DMN is anti-correlated with task-positive networks (frontoparietal, dorsal attention). DMN dysfunction in Alzheimer's (amyloid deposition), depression (hyperactivity), schizophrenia (hypoconnectivity), autism (atypical connectivity).
process_types.function: Self-referential thought, autobiographical memory, social cognition, mind-wandering, consciousness
goals: Understand brain organization as networks, not isolated regions
Connect DMN dysfunction to neuropsychiatric disorders
Appreciate resting-state networks in health and disease
process_types.energy_source: Resting-state fMRI, diffusion tractography, graph theory, clinical neuroimaging

primary_example.explanation: The DMN is selectively vulnerable in Alzheimer's disease: (1) Amyloid-β deposition occurs earliest in DMN regions (posterior cingulate, precuneus, medial prefrontal). (2) Resting-state fMRI shows reduced functional connectivity within DMN in mild cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's. (3) Hypometabolism (FDG-PET) in DMN regions correlates with cognitive decline. (4) Hypothesis: DMN neurons have high activity levels, increasing metabolic demands and vulnerability to pathology. (5) Clinical: DMN dysfunction correlates with memory impairment, visuospatial deficits. Significance: The concept of brain networks reframes understanding of neurodegenerative diseases as "connectopathies"—disorders of network integrity rather than discrete region pathology. This network perspective informs biomarker development (DMN connectivity as early marker) and potential neuromodulation targets (transcranial magnetic stimulation, deep brain stimulation).

image.alt: Default mode network regions (medial prefrontal, posterior cingulate, angular gyrus); PET showing amyloid deposition in DMN regions in Alzheimer's; resting-state fMRI showing reduced DMN connectivity.
image.caption: Default Mode Network in Alzheimer's: DMN regions show early amyloid deposition and reduced functional connectivity, linking network dysfunction to cognitive decline.

image.alt: Brain lateral and medial views showing DMN nodes (PCC, mPFC, angular gyrus, medial temporal); resting-state fMRI correlation map; activity during rest vs. task.
image.caption: Default Mode Network (DMN): Regions active during rest (mind-wandering, self-reflection). DMN deactivates during goal-directed tasks. DMN dysfunction in Alzheimer's, depression, schizophrenia.

process_types.type: White Matter Tracts and Disconnection Syndromes
process_types.energy_profile: White matter tracts (commissural, association, projection fibers) connect brain regions; lesions cause disconnection syndromes—deficits from disrupted communication between intact regions.
process_types.direction: Tract classification: Association fibers (within hemisphere—superior longitudinal fasciculus, arcuate fasciculus, uncinate fasciculus); Commissural fibers (between hemispheres—corpus callosum); Projection fibers (cortex to subcortical—internal capsule, corticospinal tract).
process_types.function: Interregional communication, distributed processing, integrated function
goals: Understand white matter anatomy and function
Appreciate role of connectivity in higher cognitive functions
process_types.energy_source: Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI), tractography, lesion studies, neuropsychology

primary_example.explanation: Corpus callosum (200 million axons) connects homologous cortical regions. Lesions cause disconnection: (1) Split-brain patients (surgical callosotomy for epilepsy): Left hemisphere (language) cannot communicate with right. Objects in left visual field (processed by right hemisphere) cannot be named (anomia) because language (left hemisphere) lacks input. (2) Left hand apraxia: Left hemisphere commands cannot reach right hemisphere for left hand movement. (3) Alien hand syndrome: Involuntary, purposeful movements of one hand (often left) that patient disowns. (4) Callosal infarction (often from ACA stroke): Acute disconnection symptoms; may recover partially. Significance: Disconnection syndromes demonstrate that higher cognitive functions require integration across distributed networks. White matter damage—often overlooked on conventional imaging—can cause profound deficits despite intact gray matter. This principle is critical for understanding traumatic brain injury (diffuse axonal injury), multiple sclerosis (demyelination), and vascular dementia (subcortical ischemic disease).

image.alt: Corpus callosum anatomy; split-brain experiment: object in left visual field cannot be named; diffusion tensor imaging showing callosal fibers; alien hand phenomenon.
image.caption: Corpus Callosum Disconnection: Split-brain patients cannot name objects presented to left visual field (right hemisphere) because language (left hemisphere) lacks input. Callosal lesions cause interhemispheric transfer deficits.

image.alt: Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) tractography showing major white matter tracts: arcuate fasciculus (language), corpus callosum (interhemispheric), corticospinal tract (motor), superior longitudinal fasciculus (attention).
image.caption: White Matter Tracts: Association fibers (within hemisphere), commissural fibers (between hemispheres), projection fibers (cortex to subcortex). DTI tractography reveals structural connectivity underlying cognitive functions.

sections.relationship: Brain function emerges from interactions within distributed networks, not isolated regions. Structural connectivity (white matter tracts) enables functional connectivity (correlated activity). Network dysfunction—disrupted connectivity—underlies many neurological and psychiatric disorders: Alzheimer's (DMN dysfunction), schizophrenia (frontotemporal dysconnectivity), autism (underconnectivity), multiple sclerosis (demyelination disrupts network integrity). Understanding brain structure as networks (connectomics) is transforming clinical neurology, providing new biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

summary: The human brain is organized into four major divisions: cerebrum (higher cognition, voluntary movement, sensory processing), cerebellum (motor coordination, balance, motor learning), brainstem (autonomic regulation, cranial nerves, arousal), and limbic system (emotion, memory, motivation). The cerebrum consists of two hemispheres with four lobes: frontal (executive function, motor planning, Broca's area—speech production), parietal (somatosensory processing, spatial awareness, Wernicke's area—language comprehension), temporal (auditory processing, memory—hippocampus, emotion—amygdala), occipital (visual processing). Cortical organization follows principles of topography (motor/sensory homunculi, retinotopy), functional specialization (language lateralized to left hemisphere), and hierarchical processing (primary → association cortex). Subcortical structures: basal ganglia (motor control circuits; Parkinson's—dopamine loss, Huntington's—striatal degeneration); thalamus (sensory relay); hypothalamus (homeostasis, endocrine control). The brainstem contains cranial nerve nuclei (CN III-XII), autonomic centers (respiratory, cardiovascular), and reticular activating system (consciousness); brainstem stroke syndromes (e.g., Wallenberg—lateral medullary) produce characteristic crossed deficits. The cerebellum coordinates movement timing and precision; damage causes ataxia, dysmetria, intention tremor. The limbic system (hippocampus—declarative memory; amygdala—emotion; cingulate—attention; Papez circuit) integrates emotion, memory, and autonomic responses. Protective structures: meninges (dura, arachnoid, pia), ventricles with CSF (hydrocephalus when obstructed). Vascular supply: anterior circulation (internal carotid → MCA, ACA) supplies cerebral hemispheres; posterior circulation (vertebrobasilar → PCA) supplies brainstem, cerebellum, occipital lobe. Stroke syndromes: MCA stroke (contralateral face/arm weakness, aphasia/neglect); ACA (contralateral leg weakness); PCA (homonymous hemianopia); brainstem (crossed findings). Modern connectomics reveals brain organization as networks (e.g., default mode network active during rest); white matter tracts (corpus callosum, arcuate fasciculus) enable distributed processing; disconnection syndromes (split-brain, conduction aphasia) demonstrate that cognition requires network integration. Understanding brain structure—from macroscopic lobes to microscopic cytoarchitecture to network connectivity—is foundational for neurology, neurosurgery, neuropsychology, and psychiatry.
